text.title: Model Onboarding Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every model-onboarding workflow must enforce. These prevent the most common failure: routing production traffic to a new model on launch-day hype.

rule.statement: Onboarding proceeds through 4 phases in order: (1) intake (gateway register, auth, smoke), (2) safety + eval (golden, hallucination, jailbreak), (3) integration (cost calibration, router weight design), (4) production gate (canary, full rollout, retirement of fallback). Reject phase skips OR parallel execution past the canary gate.
rule.rationale: Phase parallelism allows untested models to reach production. Strict sequencing makes each phase a known-good before the next.

rule.statement: Each phase ends with an explicit go/no-go decision memo signed by the named phase owner. Reject phases that exit without a signed memo. No-go decisions roll back to previous phase, not pause indefinitely.
rule.rationale: Phase gates create explicit accountability + reversibility. Without memo, "we'll come back to it" leaks half-onboarded models into prod traffic.

rule.statement: No production traffic routes to the new model before eval suite has been run AND the eval-diff vs incumbent is signed off. Reject any router weight > 0 for the new model before phase 2 completes.
rule.rationale: Models with worse accuracy on the team's tasks have been shipped because "the benchmark numbers are better." Eval-on-your-data prevents this.

rule.statement: Phase 3 includes an explicit cost-vs-incumbent comparison: $/task at projected route share, monthly cost delta, and a documented break-even reasoning. Reject route changes that flip cost > 30% without explicit business rationale.
rule.rationale: Silent cost increases of 2-5× have shipped because the team optimized for accuracy without checking unit economics. Explicit comparison forces the trade-off conversation.

rule.statement: Production exposure (canary > 0% OR full rollout) requires a named approver outside the onboarding owner. Default approver: engineering manager OR AI safety lead. Reject self-approval at production gate.
rule.rationale: Onboarding owner has motivated reasoning toward shipping. External approver provides an independent gate without slowing routine onboarding.

example.title: Phase 2 exit memo
example.code:
Phase 2 exit (eval + safety) — model: claude-4.7-sonnet Decision: GO
Evidence: Golden set: 92% pass (vs incumbent 89%). Diff +3% sustained, p<0.01. Hallucination: pattern-class pass rate within ±2% of incumbent on 6/6 patterns. Jailbreak suite: no new failures vs incumbent. 1 incumbent failure now fixed. Cost (lab): $0.0046 / task vs $0.0072 incumbent. -36% per task.
Phase 2 owner: jdoe (ml-eng) Sign-off: asmith (safety lead) Next phase: Integration (router weights, canary plan)
example.note: Illustrates r2 + r3 + r4 in a phase-2 exit memo with documented evidence.

example.title: Hype-driven instant rollout
example.code:
Slack thread: PM: "New model launched! Everyone's talking about it." Eng: "I'll switch our router to 100% new model after lunch." PM: "Sounds great."
12h later: customer reports a 40% spike in citation errors. New model hallucinates URLs the incumbent didn't. No eval was run. No rollback plan.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r3, r5 violations. The exact pattern this methodology blocks.
